coredata.dc:title: Selenium–nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots increase rice seedling growth and salt resistance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Crop Science Society of China and Institute of Crop Science, CAAS. Production and hosting by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.

Lei, Shang
Ma, Congli
Qi, Jingyi
Liu, Yingliang
Wang, Hongjie
coredata.dc:description: Soil salinity seriously affects the utilization of farmland and threatens the crop production. Here, a selenium-nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots was developed, which increased rice seedling growth and alleviated its inhibition by salt stress by foliar spraying. The treatment activated Ca2+ and jasmonic acid signaling pathways and increased iron homeostasis, antioxidant defense, and cell wall development of rice seedlings. It could be used to increase crop resistance to environmental stress.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rice seedling
Resistance
Stress signaling
Iron homeostasis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Introduction
Materials and methods
Preparation and characterization of Se,N-CDs
Rice plant cultivation and exposures to Se,N-CDs and salt stress
In vitro antioxidant and free radical scavenging activity of Se,N-CDs
Oxidative damage and antioxidant defense system of rice seedlings
Photosynthesis of rice seedlings and isolated chloroplasts
Transcriptomes of Se,N-CDs treated rice seedlings
Results
Characterization of Se,N-CDs
Se,N-CDs increased the growth and resistance of rice seedlings
Se,N-CDs increased the antioxidant ability of rice seedlings
Se,N-CDs increased photosynthesis in rice seedings
Transcriptomics profile of Se,N-CDs-treated rice seedlings
Discussion
Se,N-CDs triggered calcium and jasmonate signaling for environmental stress in rice seedlings
Se,N-CDs strengthened antioxidant defense
Se,N-CDs increased photosynthesis in rice seedlings
Se,N-CDs promoted the cell wall development of rice seedlings
Conclusions
CRediT authorship contribution statement
Acknowledgments
Supplementary data
References

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42207032, 52070064), the Key Project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (42330705), Key R&D Project of Hebei Province (21373601D), and Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University (521100222012). The authors acknowledge economic support from Collaborative Innovation Center for Baiyangdian Basin Ecological Protection and Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Sustainable Development and Institute of Life Sciences and Green Development of Hebei University. The authors also thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the NMR analysis.

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 42207032 , 52070064 ), the Key Project of National Natural Science Foundation of China ( G42330705 ), Key R&D Project of Hebei Province ( 21373601D ), and Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University ( 521100222012 ). The authors acknowledge economic support from Collaborative Innovation Center for Baiyangdian Basin Ecological Protection and Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Sustainable Development and Institute of Life Sciences and Green Development of Hebei University. The authors also thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the NMR analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Foliar spraying of Se,N-CDs improved the resistance of rice seedlings to salt stress. (A–E) The height and fresh weight of rice seedlings under salt stress and pre-sprayed rice seedlings with Se,N-CDs (50, 100, 300 μg mL−1) under salt stress (0.10 mol L−1 NaCl) as well as photographs of the seedlings; (F–I) In vitro antioxidant activity of Se,N-CDs to KMNO4 and radical scavenging activities to DPPH,
O
2
-
, and ∙OH; (J–M)
.
O
2
-
and MDA contents as well as SOD and POD activities of rice seedlings; (N–Q) ETR and Y(Ⅱ) of the leaves of rice seedlings as well as the Hill activity of chloroplast and Se,N-CDs@Chl under H2O2 stress (10 μmol L−1). Error bars indicate standard deviation (n ≥ 3). Means marked with different letters are different (P < 0.05).

The up-regulated differentially expressed genes (DEGs) of rice seedlings by Se, N-CDs. Bubble diagram of GO (A) and KEGG (B) enrichment of the up-regulated DEGs showing the top 20 enriched GO term/KEGG pathways; Detail fold changes (Se,N-CDs/Control) of the up-regulated DEGs in alpha-Linolenic acid/Linoleic acid metabolism and iron homeostasis (C), antioxidants (peroxidase and L-ascorbate oxidase) (D), cell wall development and plant-pathogen interaction (E).

head.ce:title: Selenium–nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots increase rice seedling growth and salt resistance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Ecological Safety and Conservation in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (Xiong’an New Area) of MOE, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Ecological Safety and Conservation in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (Xiong’an New Area) of MOE

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Ecological Safety and Conservation in Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei (Xiong’an New Area) of MOE, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Soil salinity seriously affects the utilization of farmland and threatens the crop production. Here, a selenium-nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots was developed, which increased rice seedling growth and alleviated its inhibition by salt stress by foliar spraying. The treatment activated Ca2+ and jasmonic acid signaling pathways and increased iron homeostasis, antioxidant defense, and cell wall development of rice seedlings. It could be used to increase crop resistance to environmental stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental stresses such as soil salinity limit crop production [1], typically by disrupting redox reactions in plant cells, leading to reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulation and oxidative damage. Reducing ROS accumulation and strengthening plant immune systems are targets for crop improvement.

Recently, various novel applications of nanomaterials in plant protection against abiotic stress have been described [2]. Carbon dots (CDs) is a new kind of carbon-based nanomaterials with excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low cost and has been widely demonstrated to increased plant photosynthesis, resistance, and growth [3]. For the interaction between plants and environmental stresses, CDs could scavenge ROS radicals and improved plant antioxidant defense [4], calcium mobilization [5], and nutrient metabolism [6,7]. Recently, heteroatom doping was increasingly used to further increase the radical scavenging activity of CDs by introducing electron donor and acceptor structures [8,9].

Selenium (Se), with excellent redox activity, can stabilize the immune system and activate the defense responses in biological systems [10]. The objective of this study was to develop Se and nitrogen co-doped CDs (Se,N-CDs) and evaluate the effect of their foliar application on rice growth and salt-stress response. The in vitro antioxidant activity of Se,N-CDs as well as the antioxidant defense, photosynthesis, and transcriptome of the treated rice seedlings were measured to understand the functional mechanism of Se,N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Preparation and characterization of Se,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Se,N-CDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. Citric acid (2.1 g), ethanol amine (242 μL), and seleninic acid (0.4 g) were mixed in 20 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C in an oven for 6 h. The resulting liquid was filtered through a syringe filter (0.22 μm), dialyzed using cellulose ester dialysis membranes (MW 100–500) against ultrapure water for 12 h, and dried in a vacuum freeze dryer. N-doped CDs (N-CDs) were similarly prepared using citric acid and ethanol amine without seleninic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rice plant cultivation and exposures to Se,N-CDs and salt stress
ce:section.ce:para: Rice seedlings (Huahang 31) were cultured hydroponically (in nutrient solution described in Table S1) in a greenhouse (22–30 °C, 70% relative humidity, 400 μmol m−2 s−1 light intensity with a 16 h light/8 h dark regime), and the nutrient solution was refreshed twice a week [11]. Se,N-CDs solutions (0, 50, 100, and 300 μg mL−1) were sprayed on the seedlings immediately after transplantation once daily for one week at 5 mL pot−1. Salt stress was applied by adding NaCl (0, 0.05, 0.10, and 0.15 mol L−1) to the nutrient solutions one week after transplantation. Both individual and combined treatments of Se,N-CDs and salt stress were applied. Each treatment was replicated three times. The height and fresh weights of rice seedlings were measured. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the distribution of Se,N-CDs in seedlings. Se,N-CDs at 300 μg mL−1 and NaCl at 0,1 mol L−1 were used in subsequent tests of rice seedlings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antioxidant and free radical scavenging activity of Se,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
KMnO4 reduction and DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) radical scavenging assays of Se,N-CDs (0, 4, 8, 12, 16, and 20 μg mL−1) followed a previously reported method with modifications were shown in Experiment S1 [12].
.
O
2
-
radical scavenging activity of Se,N-CDs (0, 4, 8, 12, 16, and 20 μg mL−1) was studied using an superoxide dismutase (SOD) kit (Shanghai Cablebridge Biotechnology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China). Three replicates were measured for each treatment. The hydroxyl radical (∙OH) scavenging activity of Se,N-CDs was assessed by electron spin resonance spectroscopy, in which ∙OH was produced by Fenton reaction and DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide) was used as the trapping agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative damage and antioxidant defense system of rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para:
The contents of H2O2,
.
O
2
-
, and malonaldehyde (MDA), and the activities of SOD, peroxidase (POD), and catalase (CAT) in rice seedlings were measured with kits (Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Suzhou, Jiangsu, China). Three biological replicates were measured for each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthesis of rice seedlings and isolated chloroplasts
ce:section.ce:para: Electron transport rate (ETR) and photochemical energy conversion [Y(II)] quantum yield of photosystem II (PS II) in rice leaves were measured with a pulse-amplitude modulated (PAM) chlorophyll fluorescence imaging system (MAGING-PAM, Walz, Germany). Before measurement, seedlings were dark-adapted for 60 min. Photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) for the measurement ranged from 0 to 1251 μmol m−2 s−1. Ten points on the second leaves of four rice seedlings from each treatment were measured.

Chloroplasts were isolated from lettuce (Lactuca sativa L.) leaves [13]. The chloroplasts (10 μg mL−1) were modified with Se,N-CDs (20 and 40 μg mL−1) (Se,N-CDs@Chl) by co-incubation in the dark at 4 °C for 1 h. The fluorescence lifetimes of Se,N-CDs alone and in Se,N-CDs@Chl were measured to characterize the resonance energy transfer between Se,N-CDs and chloroplasts. Hill reactions under H2O2 stress were conducted to measure the protection by Se,N-CDs of photosynthetic activity in the chloroplast against oxidative stress, using 2,6-dichlorophenolindophenol (DCPIP) and potassium ferricyanide as electron acceptors (Experiment S2). A chloroplast suspension without H2O2 was measured as a control, and three replications were evaluated for each treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptomes of Se,N-CDs treated rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Six fresh rice leaf samples from control and Se,N-CDs at 300 μg mL−1 treatments were collected, flash-frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored at −80 °C. TRIzol Reagent (Plant RNA Purification Reagent for plant tissue) was used to extract and purify the total RNA following the manufacturer’s instructions (Invitrogen). High-quality RNA samples (mass ≥ 1 μg, concentration ≥ 35 ng μL−1, OD260/280 ≥ 1.8, OD260/230 ≥ 1.0, RIN ≥ 6.5) were reverse transcribed into cDNA for constructing a sequencing library. The cDNA library was sequenced on an Illumina HiSeq xten/NovaSeq 6000 sequencer (2 × 150 nt read length). The expression levels of genes and transcripts and the principal component analysis (PCA) between the control and Se,N-CDs treatments were carried out using RSEM software (version 1.3.3, https://deweylab.biostat.wisc.edu/rsem/) using the index of TPM (transcripts per million reads) and FPKM (fragments per kilobases per million reads). Differentially expressed genes (DEGs) between the control and Se,N-CDs treatments were identified using DESeq2 software (version 1.10.1, https://bioconductor.org/packages/stats/bioc/DESeq2/), with an absolute fold change ≥1.5 and corrected P-value ≤0.05. The function annotation and enrichment of DEGs were conducted based on GO (Gene Ontology, https://www.geneontology.org/) and KEGG (Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes, https://www.genome.jp/kegg/) databases. The quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction was used to verify the result of transcriptome (Experiment S3; Fig. S4; Table S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Characterization of Se,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared Se,N-CDs were monodisperse particles with an average size of 3.06 nm and a lattice fringe of 0.20 nm. C, N, O, and Se elements were evenly distributed in Se,N-CDs (Fig. S1A–F). Se,N-CDs showed maximum light absorption, fluorescence excitation, and fluorescence emission around 316, 360, and 471 nm, respectively (Fig. S1G, H). The fluorescence lifetime and relative quantum yield of Se,N-CDs was measured as 1.39 ns and 31.65%, respectively (Fig. S1I). FTIR, XPS, and NMR spectra of Se,N-CDs suggested that there are various hydrophilic chemical structures with defects and unpaired electrons on Se,N-CDs (Figs. S2, S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se,N-CDs increased the growth and resistance of rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Under normal conditions, foliar application of Se,N-CDs significantly promoted the growth of rice in a concentration-dependent manner. Se,N-CDs at 100 and 300 μg mL−1 led to similar promotion to the plant height and fresh weight of rice seedlings (Fig. S5). Se,N-CDs also significantly increased the chlorophyll content in rice leaves (Fig. S6). To the contrary, salt stresses of 0.05 mol L−1, 0.10 mol L−1, and 0.15 mol L−1 NaCl resulted in significant decreases in the plant height and fresh weight of rice seedlings (Fig. 1 A, B). However, the growth of rice seedlings pre-sprayed with Se,N-CDs for a week showed negligible influence by salt stress (0.10 mol L−1 NaCl) (Fig. 1C–E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se,N-CDs increased the antioxidant ability of rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para:
Se,N-CDs showed high antioxidant activity towards KMnO4 and scavenging activity against DPPH and
∙
O
2
-
radicals in a dose-dependent manner, exceeding the activity of N-CDs (Fig. 1F–H). Se,N-CDs also showed scavenging activity of ∙OH radicals in the EPR measurement (Fig. 1I). In rice seedlings, salt stress (0.10 mol L−1 NaCl) increased
∙ O
2
-
and MDA contents and decreased SOD and POD activities, respectively, compared to the control group. However, in rice seedlings pre-sprayed with Se,N-CDs under salt stress, no significant effects on
∙ O
2
-
and MDA contents as well as SOD and POD activities were observed (Fig. 1J–M). H2O2 content and CAT activity in rice seedlings were not affected by salt stress and Se,N-CDs (Fig. S7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se,N-CDs increased photosynthesis in rice seedings
ce:section.ce:para: After spraying, Se,N-CDs were uniformly distributed in the cytoplasm and chloroplasts of rice leaves without obvious changes in cell structures (Fig. S8A, B). Under normal conditions, the ETR and Y(Ⅱ) of rice leaves were increased by Se,N-CDs (300 μg mL−1), with maximum increases of 44.70% and 46.70% at 531 μmol m−2 s−1, respectively (Fig. S8C, D). Rice seedlings under salt stress showed significant decreases in ETR and Y(Ⅱ) compared to the control group, which were completely alleviated by pre-spraying of Se,N-CDs (Fig. 1N, O). In the isolated chloroplast, the fluorescence lifetime of Se,N-CDs decreased from 1.39 ns to 0.91 ns (Fig. S8E), indicating the resonance energy transfer from Se,N-CDs to chloroplasts. In an oxidative environment simulated by H2O2, the Hill activity of chloroplasts showed concentration-dependent decreases (Fig. S8F). The combination with Se,N-CDs significantly relieved the inhibition of H2O2 stress (10 μmol L−1) on the Hill activity of chloroplast to reduce DCPIP and ferricyanide (Fig. 1P, Q). Thus, Se,N-CDs in rice leaves provided more photosynthetically active radiation and increased the photosynthetic activity of the chloroplasts. Besides, Se,N-CDs also protected the photosynthesis against oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transcriptomics profile of Se,N-CDs-treated rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 22,966 genes were detected in rice seedlings, of which respectively 525 and 421 genes were expressed specifically in the control and Se,N-CDs treatments (Fig. S9A). Spraying of Se,N-CDs induced 191 up-regulated and 190 down-regulated DEGs (Fig. S9B). Se,N-CDs induced minor alterations to the transcriptome profile of rice seedlings due to the insignificant separation between the control and Se,N-CDs groups in PCA plot (Fig. S9C). The up-regulated DEGs and all DEGs showed similar function enrichments in GO terms and KEGG pathways (Figs. 2 A, B, S10), and no GO terms and KEGG pathways were enriched by the down-regulated DEGs. Up-regulated GO terms by spraying of Se,N-CDs were associated with sequestering and maintenance of iron ions in cell, extracellular regions, phenylpropanoid, and secondary metabolite biosynthetic processes (Fig. 2A; Table S3). Up-regulated KEGG pathways by Se,N-CDs included plant-pathogen interaction, alpha-linolenic acid metabolism, phenylpropanoid biosynthesis, and linoleic acid metabolism (Fig. 2B; Table S4). Several up-regulated genes (gene ID: Os07g0129300, Os03g0262150, Os01g0699450, Os11g0600500, and Os01g0382400) were enriched in the MAPK signaling pathway (Table S4). All these GO terms and KEGG pathways are associated with stress and immune responses in plants, including stress perception, signal transduction, and resistance responses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared Se,N-CDs showed ultrasmall particle size, photoluminescence, hydrophilic surface chemical structures, and Se doping. These characteristics contributed to uptake and transport in plants, light conversion for plant photosynthesis, and interaction with ROS radicals in plants. After foliar spraying, Se,N-CDs increased the growth and resistance of rice seedlings to salt stress. The underlying mechanism was discussed as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se,N-CDs triggered calcium and jasmonate signaling for environmental stress in rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Both calcium ions (Ca2+) and jasmonic acid are ubiquitous secondary messengers in plants that transduce stress signals and induce resistance response. Spraying of Se,N-CDs up-regulated the gene expressions related to Ca2+ and jasmonate signaling in rice, including the calcium-binding EF-hand domain-containing protein gene (ID: Os11g0600500) in plant-pathogen interaction and MAPK signaling pathways, as well as the lipoxygenase gene (OsLOX11, ID: Os12g0559200), lipoxygenase-like gene (ID: Os12g0559934), and OsJMT1 gene (ID: Os05g0102000) in the alpha-linolenic acid and linoleic acid metabolism pathways (Fig. 2C; Table S4). Calcium-binding EF-hand domain-containing proteins in plants function to sense and buffer cellular Ca2+ changes and regulate downstream signaling transduction and adaptive responses [14]. Both lipoxygenases and OsJMT1 genes are involved in the biosynthesis of jasmonate [15,16]. Thus, foliar spraying of Se,N-CDs improved the capacity of rice to sense and signal stress through Ca2+ and jasmonate signaling pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se,N-CDs strengthened antioxidant defense
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental stress usually induces ROS accumulation in plants that causes oxidative damages to cellular structure and components and affects plant growth [17]. In this study, the prepared Se,N-CDs exhibited excellent antioxidant activity and enhanced the antioxidant defense system of rice seedlings, thereby synergistically increasing the antioxidant capacity of rice seedlings under salt stress. The antioxidant activity of Se,N-CDs could be attributed to its N-, O-containing moieties and the doped Se (Fig. S11). And the enhanced antioxidant defense system in rice seedlings by Se,N-CDs could be explained by the decreased ROS production and increased ROS scavenging.

Iron is an essential cofactor for various enzymes that catalyze redox reactions in biological systems and determines the production of hydroxyl radical (∙OH) via Fenton reaction in plants [18]. In Se,N-CDs-sprayed rice seedlings, two ferritin genes FER2 (ID: Os12g0106000) and OsFER1 (ID: Os11g0106700) [19], a Vacuolar Iron Transporter (VIT) family gene OsVIT2 (ID: Os09g0396900) [20], and two other iron homeostasis-related genes OsPRF1 (ID: Os10g0323600) and a similar to Nodulin 21 (ID: Os04g0538400) [21,22] were up-regulated (Fig. 2C; Table S3). A fluorescent probe (FerroOrange, MKbio) was used to verify the iron homeostasis in rice cells (Fig. S12). NaCl stress induced significant fluorescent signal distributions in both rice roots and shoots, compared to the control and Se,N-CDs-sprayed rice seedlings. However, under NaCl stress conditions, no obvious fluorescent signal was observed in rice seedlings that were pre-sprayed with Se,N-CDs for one week. The improved iron homeostasis contributed to the redox homeostasis in rice seedlings and controlling ROS production.

Se,N-CDs exposure up-regulated several genes associated with antioxidants in rice seedlings, including peroxidase (POD, EC 1.11.1.7) and L-ascorbate oxidase (AO, EC 1.10.3.3) [23,24]. Rice seedlings pre-sprayed with Se,N-CDs showed higher gene expressions related to the biosynthesis and catalysis of POD (PRX104, ID: Os07g0531400; PRX78, ID: Os06g0306300; PRX109, ID: Os07g0676900; PRX39, ID: Os03g0234900; PRX42, ID: Os03g0368000; PRX41, ID: Os03g0339300) and AO (ID: Os06g0567900) (Fig. 2D; Table S3). In addition, the improved phenylpropanoid biosynthesis pathway of rice by Se,N-CDs (Fig. 2A, B; Tables S3, S4) would contribute to rice development and their interaction with the environment [25]. Therefore, with the inhibited ROS production and increased ROS scavenging, Se,N-CDs-pre-sprayed rice seedlings suffered from much weaker oxidative damage under salt stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se,N-CDs increased photosynthesis in rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: In rice seedlings, the photoluminescence of Se,N-CDs converted UV radiation into photosynthetically active radiation, which was then harvested by the increased chlorophyll for photosynthetic electron transfer in rice leaves. Moreover, Se,N-CDs in rice seedlings also protected the photosynthetic apparatus from oxidative damage due to their antioxidant and radical scavenging activity. In plants, up to 80% of the iron is located in chloroplasts as components of the photosynthetic apparatus and electron donor and acceptor in photosynthetic electron transfer reactions [26]. The improved ferritin and VIT family genes in rice seedlings by Se,N-CDs modulated the iron sequestration for rapid remodeling of impaired photosynthetic apparatus under environmental stresses [19]. The increase in photosynthesis by Se,N-CDs contributed to rice resistance against salt stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Se,N-CDs promoted the cell wall development of rice seedlings
ce:section.ce:para: Exposure to Se,N-CDs up-regulated some genes associated with cell wall development. The OsEXP3 (ID: Os05g0276500) and OsEXPB18 (ID: Os05g0246300) are involved in the anatomical structure and morphogenesis of the cell wall. The OsCER4 and OsRALF-2 control the biosynthesis of suberin. And a carboxylic ester hydrolase similar gene (ID: Os01g0892500) is associated with pectin acetylesterase activity (Fig. 2E; Table S3) [27,28]. These up-regulated genes contributed to the cell wall construction and increased the mechanical support of rice plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: Crop growth and resistance are important for food production. This study developed a Se,N-CDs aiming to improve rice growth and resistance in agriculture. Foliar spraying of Se,N-CDs triggered the Ca2+ and jasmonic acid signaling pathways in rice seedlings, which induced improvements in ROS equilibrium, antioxidant defense, photosynthesis, and cell wall development. These influences finally conferred rice seedlings higher growth and resistance to salt stress. This study presents a simple and effective strategy for engineering plants to withstand adverse environmental conditions and contributes to global food production and safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Yadong Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ronghua Xu: Data curation, Investigation. Qianying Han: Data curation, Investigation. Shang Lei: Data curation, Investigation. Congli Ma: Methodology, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Jingyi Qi: Data curation, Investigation. Yingliang Liu: Resources, Supervision. Hongjie Wang: Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42207032, 52070064), the Key Project of National Natural Science Foundation of China (42330705), Key R&D Project of Hebei Province (21373601D), and Advanced Talents Incubation Program of the Hebei University (521100222012). The authors acknowledge economic support from Collaborative Innovation Center for Baiyangdian Basin Ecological Protection and Beijing-Tianjin-Hebei Sustainable Development and Institute of Life Sciences and Green Development of Hebei University. The authors also thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the NMR analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data for this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cj.2024.06.014

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:section-title: Supplementary data
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Designing salt stress-resilient crops: current progress and future challenges
J. Integr. Plant Biol.
